title: Roast Pork Loin with Crackling
subtitle: Scored, salted pork loin with blistered crackling and a proper roasting-tin gravy
description: A British Sunday roast centred on a bone-in pork loin with the rind on. The technique is straightforward — score the rind deeply, dry it overnight in the fridge, then blast it with very high heat before dropping the temperature to finish the joint. Done correctly, the crackling blisters and shatters; the meat beneath stays juicy. The pan drippings make a lean, savoury gravy with no thickening needed.
sourceNotes: The dry-overnight and high-initial-blast method for crackling appears in Jane Grigson's Charcuterie and French Pork Cookery (1967) and is standard in British Sunday-roast tradition. No single canonical primary source; technique is consistent across British cookery writing from the mid-twentieth century onward.

recipeTools.notes: Large enough to hold the joint with room around it for the onions and stock. A rack inside the tin lifts the joint clear of the drippings.
Or a Stanley knife — the key is cutting through the rind without scoring the fat or flesh.
Essential. Pork loin is done at 68–70 °C internal. Without a thermometer, the timing is guesswork.
For tenting the joint during resting.

glossaryTerms.definition: The outer skin of a pork joint. When deeply scored, dried, and exposed to high heat it blisters into crackling — brittle, salty, and rich with rendered fat.
Leaving cooked meat off the heat before carving. Juices redistribute back through the muscle fibres rather than running out onto the board.

ingredients.notes: Ask the butcher to score the rind at 1 cm intervals, or do it yourself with a sharp knife
Halved, unpeeled — they support the joint and flavour the gravy
Or water; added to the tin to prevent the drippings burning

steps: Score the rind: use a sharp knife to cut lines 1 cm apart through the rind and into the fat, but not into the flesh. Pat the rind completely dry with kitchen paper.

Mix the flaky salt with the fennel seeds and rub it hard into the score lines, pressing as much salt into the cuts as possible. Leave the joint uncovered on a rack in the fridge overnight, or for at least 4 hours. The drier the surface, the better the crackling.
When ready to cook, heat the oven to 240 °C fan (260 °C / gas 9). Remove the joint from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking.
Place the onion halves cut-side down in the roasting tin to form a rack. Rest the pork on top, rind-side up. Drizzle the olive oil over the rind.
Roast at 240 °C fan for 20–25 minutes. The rind should visibly blister and crackle. Watch through the oven door; if it colours unevenly, rotate the tin.

Once the rind has crackled, reduce the oven to 180 °C fan (200 °C / gas 6). Pour the stock into the tin — around the joint, not over the rind. Roast for a further 60–70 minutes, or until a thermometer reads 68–70 °C at the thickest part of the meat away from the bone.
Transfer the joint to a warm board and tent loosely with foil. Rest for 20 minutes.

Meanwhile, make the gravy: pour the tin drippings into a small pan, discarding the onions. Skim off the majority of the fat. Set over a medium heat and, if you want a slightly thicker gravy, whisk in the tablespoon of flour and cook for 1 minute before adding any extra stock. Taste and season.
Carve through the crackling between the score lines — use a strong, sharp knife. Serve immediately, crackling placed on top of each slice.

content.text: The overnight dry is not optional. Moisture is the enemy of crackling — if the

is even slightly damp when it enters the oven, the high heat turns the moisture to steam and the surface puffs unevenly rather than blistering all over. Uncovered in the fridge overnight draws out any residual moisture and allows the surface to desiccate. Four hours minimum if overnight is not possible.

If the butcher has not already scored the rind, do it now. Cut lines 1 cm apart with a sharp knife, cutting through the rind and into the fat beneath but stopping before the flesh. Pat the surface completely dry with kitchen paper. Mix 2 tsp of flaky salt with lightly crushed fennel seeds and black pepper, then rub it hard into the score lines — press the salt deep into the cuts. Place the joint uncovered on a rack in the fridge for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.

Heat the oven to 240 °C fan. Remove the joint from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Place two halved onions cut-side down in the roasting tin and rest the pork on top, rind-side up. Drizzle 1 tbsp of olive oil over the rind. Roast for 20–25 minutes — the rind should blister and crackle audibly. Rotate the tin if it colours unevenly.

Reduce the oven to 180 °C fan. Pour {{chicken-stock}} of stock into the tin around the joint, not over it. Roast for a further 60–70 minutes, checking with a thermometer: 68–70 °C at the thickest point away from the bone. Transfer to a warm board and tent with foil.
time. Do not skip this — the internal temperature will rise a degree or two and the juices will redistribute.

Pour the tin drippings into a small pan, discarding the softened onions. Skim off most of the fat — leave a tablespoon for flavour. If you want a slightly thicker consistency, whisk in 1 tbsp of plain flour and cook for 1 minute before adding extra stock. Season and keep warm.

To carve, cut downward between the crackling score lines with a strong, sharp knife — the crackling should snap cleanly if it has blistered properly. Lay a piece of crackling on top of each slice of meat.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Crackling is the part that tends to go wrong — here is why.

attrs.problem: Crackling is chewy rather than crisp
attrs.solution: The rind was not dry enough, or the oven was not hot enough at the start. Pat the rind dry again and return the joint to the oven at 240 °C fan for 10 minutes, watching closely, to try to rescue it. Next time, ensure a full overnight dry in the fridge.
It has overcooked past 75 °C. Use a thermometer next time and pull the joint at 68–70 °C. Pork loin has very little fat and dries out quickly above this temperature.

attrs.problem: Crackling blistered in patches but not all over
attrs.solution: The surface was uneven — either the scoring was inconsistent or the oven has a hot spot. Rotate the tin halfway through the high-heat blast. Even, deep scoring with consistent 1 cm spacing also helps.

content.text: Peel, core, and roughly chop 3 Bramley apples. Cook them in a covered pan with a splash of water and a pinch of sugar over a low heat for 10–15 minutes until collapsed. Beat smooth with a wooden spoon. The sharpness cuts through the richness of the pork and crackling.
